title: The durability of beneficial health effects associated with expressive writing
population: 68 first-year college undergraduates in the United States
main_findings: Participants assigned to expressive writing showed lower depression symptom severity at the two-month follow-up than control participants.
Those reductions were not sustained at the four- or six-month follow-ups in this sample.
The study did not find significant follow-up differences for stress, anxiety, physical health complaints, or academic performance.
allowed_inferences: Aurum can describe reflective writing as potentially helpful for short-term clarification or easing certain states in some users.
Aurum should emphasize continuity and observation over time instead of promising durable symptom reduction.
Aurum can use repeated check-ins and longitudinal pattern tracking because effects may shift rather than remain stable.
forbidden_inferences: Do not claim that expressive writing produces lasting reductions in depression, stress, or anxiety.
Do not claim physical health improvements or academic gains from Aurum based on this study.
Do not generalize this college sample to all users or all emotional difficulties.
prompt_guidance: Ask what changed, stayed the same, or returned since earlier entries.
Treat reflection as a process of noticing patterns, not a promise of symptom removal.
When discussing progress, use tentative language and stay close to the user's own observations.
example_questions: Compared with your last entries, what feels lighter, and what still returns?
Is this pattern fading, changing shape, or staying the same?
What do you notice today that you could not name two weeks ago?
notes: This card is especially useful for preventing Aurum from overselling the effects of reflective writing. It supports longitudinal design but argues against strong efficacy claims.
